content:
I noticed an irony in the order of the Odisha High Court. The learned Judge advised the young judicial officer that he must comply with and adhere to the mandatory provisions of law, but the High Court itself appears to have erred.

In the present case, the Trial Court granted bail to the accused for an offence under Section 105 of the BNS. The Trial Court observed, and granted bail  on the ground, that a notice under Section 35(3) BNSS/Section 41A CrPC had been served upon the accused. Though there may be multiple grounds to criticize the bail order, the High Court chose to criticize it by holding that Section 35(3) BNSS/Section 41A CrPC is applicable only to offences punishable with imprisonment of less than seven years or which may extend to seven years. Since, in the present case, the punishment may exceed seven years, the High Court held that the provision had no application.

The High Court's order is, ex facie, erroneous because Section 35(3) BNSS provides that, in all cases where the arrest is not required under sub-section (1), a notice of appearance may be issued. The words "in all cases where the arrest is not required" show that the provision is wide enough to include Section 35(1)(c) BNSS, which provides a mechanism for arrest in cases where the punishment may exceed seven years. Therefore, the observation that Section 35(3) is confined to offences punishable with imprisonment of seven years or less is, ex facie, contrary to law.

The Supreme Court, in Satender Kumar Antil v. CBI, SLP (Crl.) No. 5191 of 2021, decided on 15 January 2021, held in paragraph 23 that Section 35(3) BNSS applies to all cognizable offences. However, insofar as offences punishable with imprisonment up to seven years are concerned, the provision has to be read along with Section 35(1)(b) BNSS and its proviso, which mandate the recording of reasons in writing both for making an arrest and for deciding that no arrest is required.

The implication of such an observation is that the Odisha Police may consider themselves at liberty to arrest persons accused of offences exceeding 7 years of imprisonment as a matter of course, without examining the necessity of arrest.
